title: Filing status and form
description: Practice understanding of filing federal income tax returns, understanding filing status, and common misconceptions.

assessment_items.question_content:
A tax filing status helps determine which benefits you qualify for and how much tax you may owe. 

**Identify the correct filing status for each scenario below.**

1. If you are a single parent with children, your filing status is [[☃ dropdown 1]]. 

2. You and your spouse are both retired and living at home. Your filing status is [[☃ dropdown 2]].
hints:
If you are single, you might qualify for one of the following statuses:  

- Single  
- Head of household  
- Qualifying surviving spouse (if your spouse recently passed away)

A single *parent* with children typically qualifies for Head of household status, but can also file as a Qualifying surviving spouse if they are a widow(er). Both of these offer specific tax benefits over filing as Single.

If you are married, you generally choose between:  

- Married filing jointly, or  
- Married filing separately
Married filing separately status makes sense in a select few scenarios, like if one spouse has high medical bills or if the couple is going through a divorce.

If you are a single parent with children, your filing status is $\boxed{\text{head of the household}}$. 

You and your spouse are both retired and living at home. Your filing status is $\boxed{\text{married filing jointly}}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A tax filing status helps determine which benefits you qualify for and how much tax you may owe. 

**Identify the correct filing status for each scenario below.**

1. You and your spouse both work full-time jobs and occupy one house. Your filing status is [[☃ dropdown 1]].

2. You are a young adult, and your younger siblings live with you. Your filing status is [[☃ dropdown 2]].
hints:
If you are married, you might qualify for one of the following statuses:  

- Married filing jointly, or 
- Married filing separately

If you are a single person taking care of your younger siblings, you generally choose between:  

- Single, or  
- Head of household
A married couple that combines their incomes on one tax return usually pays less in taxes than each person filing separately.
The head of household status allows unmarried individuals to pay less tax while they take care of dependents.

You and your spouse both work full-time jobs and occupy one house. Your filing status is $\boxed{\text{married filing jointly}}$. 

You are a young adult, and your younger siblings live with you. Your filing status is $\boxed{\text{head of household}}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A tax filing status helps determine which benefits you qualify for and how much tax you may owe. 

**Identify the correct filing status for each scenario below.**

1. A person whose spouse passed away recently would file taxes using the [[☃ dropdown 1]] status.

2. You are a single grandparent who provides for a grandchild living with you. Your filing status is [[☃ dropdown 2]].
hints:
A person whose spouse recently passed away qualifies for one of the following statuses:  

- Single
- Qualifying surviving spouse

If you are a single grandparent taking care of your grandchild full-time, you generally choose between:  

- Single, or  
- Head of household
A person whose spouse recently passed away will benefit from the qualifying surviving spouse option when filing taxes, because it allows them to keep joint tax benefits for two years.
The head of household option allows unmarried individuals to pay less tax because they take care of dependents.

A person whose spouse passed away recently would file taxes using the $\boxed{\text{qualifying surviving spouse}}$ status. 

You are a single grandparent who provides for a grandchild living with you. Your filing status is $\boxed{\text{head of household}}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Most people who work in the US need to file a tax return if they make more than a certain amount for the year.

**Consider the partial income threshold table and complete the statement below:**

|| **Tax year 2023 filing thresholds by filing status** ||  
Filing status | Age at the end of 2023 | Income threshold  
- | - | -  
Single | Under 65 | $\$13{,}850$  
Head of household | Under 65 | $\$20{,}800$
Married filing jointly | 65 or older | $\$30{,}700$    

If you and your spouse had $\$29{,}800$ in combined income, then you [[☃ dropdown 1]] file an income tax return.
hints: First, you must identify the **filing status** that applies to your situation, and then reference the income threshold.
Since you and your spouse have a combined income, your filing status is **Married filing jointly** and the income threshold for that status is $\$30{,}700$ .

The income threshold amount refers to the minimum income that requires you to file a tax return. 
The combined income of $\$29{,}800$ is *less* than the threshold income, so you are not required to file a tax return. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]
If you and your spouse had $\$29{,}800$ in combined income, then you $\boxed{\text{don't have to}}$ file an income tax return.

assessment_items.question_content:
Most people who work in the US need to file a tax return if they make more than a certain amount for the year.

**Consider the partial income threshold table and complete the statement below:**

|| **Tax year 2023 filing thresholds by filing status** ||  
Filing status | Age at the end of 2023 | Income threshold  
- | - | -  
Single | 65 or older | $\$15{,}700$  
Head of household | 65 or over | $\$22{,}650$
Married filing separately | Any age | $\$5$    

If you are $67$ years old, not married,  with no dependents, and have an income of $\$13{,}700$, then you [[☃ dropdown 1]] file an income tax return.
hints: First, you must identify the **filing status** that applies to your situation, and then reference the income threshold.
Since you are unmarried with no dependents, your filing status is **Single** and the income threshold for those over 65 with that status is $\$15{,}700$ .

The income threshold amount refers to the minimum income that requires you to file a tax return. 
Your income of $\$13{,}700$ is *less* than the threshold income, so you are not required to file a tax return. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]
If you are $67$ years old, not married,  with no dependents, and have an income of $\$13{,}700$, then you $\boxed{\text{don't have to}}$ file an income tax return.

assessment_items.question_content:
Most people who work in the US need to file a tax return if they make more than a certain amount for the year.

**Consider the partial income threshold table and complete the statement below:**

|| **Tax year 2023 filing thresholds by filing status** ||  
Filing status | Age at the end of 2023 | Income threshold  
- | - | -  
Single | Under 65 | $\$13{,}850^*$  
Head of household | Under 65 | $\$20{,}800^*$
Married filing jointly | Under 65 | $\$27{,}700^*$    

$^*$ *Self-employed individuals must file a tax return if their net earnings are $\$400$ or more.*


If you are self-employed, not married, supporting a grandson, and have an income of $\$19{,}900$, then you [[☃ dropdown 1]] file an income tax return.
hints: First, you must identify the **filing status** that applies to your situation, and then reference the income threshold.

Since you are an unmarried person with a dependent, your filing status is **Head of household**, and the income threshold with that status is $\$20{,}800$. But since you are *self-employed*, your actual income threshold is $\$400$.

The income threshold amount refers to the minimum income that requires you to file a tax return. 
Your income of $\$19{,}900$ is *more* than the threshold income for self-employed individuals, so you are required to file a tax return.
If you are self-employed, not married, supporting a grandson, and have an income of  $\$19{,}900$, then you $\boxed{\text{must}}$ file an income tax return.

assessment_items.question_content:
Most people who work in the US need to file a tax return if they make more than a certain amount for the year.

**Consider the partial income threshold table and complete the statement below:**

|| **Tax year 2023 filing thresholds by filing status** ||  
Filing status | Age at the end of 2023 | Income threshold  
- | - | -  
Married filing jointly | 65 or older (one spouse) | $\$29{,}200$  
Single | 65 or older | $\$15{,}700$
Qualifying surviving spouse | Under 65 | $\$27{,}700$    

A person whose spouse recently passed away and is also caring for their son, on an income of $\$38{,}200$, [[☃ dropdown 1]] file an income tax return.
hints: First, you must identify the **filing status** that applies to the situation and then reference the income threshold.
Since the person is recently widowed and caring for a dependent, the filing status is **Qualifying surviving spouse** and the income threshold for that status is $\$27{,}700$.

The income threshold amount refers to the minimum income that requires a person to file a tax return. 
An income of $\$38{,}200$ is *more* than the threshold income, so a person is required to file a tax return.
A person whose spouse recently passed away and is also caring for their son, on an income of  $\$38{,}200$, $\boxed{\text{must}}$ file an income tax return.

assessment_items.question_content:
Most people who work in the US need to file a tax return if they make more than a certain amount for the year.

**Consider the partial income threshold table and complete the statement below:**

|| **Tax year 2023 filing thresholds by filing status** ||  
Filing status | Age at the end of 2023 | Income threshold  
- | - | -  
Head of household | 65 or older | $\$22{,}650$  
Single | Under 65 | $\$13{,}850$
Married filing jointly | Under 65 | $\$27{,}700$    

If you and your spouse had $\$58{,}600$ in combined income, then you  [[☃ dropdown 1]] file an income tax return.
hints: First, you must identify the **filing status** that applies to your situation, and then reference the income threshold.
Since you and your spouse have a combined income , your filing status is **Married filing jointly** and the income threshold for that status is $\$27{,}700$.

The income threshold amount refers to the minimum income that requires you to file a tax return. 
Your income of $\$58{,}600$ is *more* than the threshold income, so you are required to file a tax return.
If your and your spouse had $\$58{,}600$ in combined income, then you $\boxed{\text{must}}$ file an income tax return.

assessment_items.question_content:
Most people who work in the US need to file a tax return if they make more than a certain amount for the year.

**Consider the partial income threshold table and complete the statement below:**

|| **Tax year 2023 filing thresholds by filing status** ||  
Filing status | Age at the end of 2023 | Income threshold  
- | - | -  
Head of household | Under 65 | $\$20{,}800$  
Married filing separately| Any age | $\$5$
Qualifying surviving spouse | Under 65 | $\$27{,}700$    

If you are an unmarried adult, raising your younger sibling and make  $\$18{,}200$, then you  [[☃ dropdown 1]] file an income tax return.
hints: First, you must identify the **filing status** that applies to your situation, and then reference the income threshold.
Since you are unmarried and caring for a dependent, your filing status is **Head of household** and the income threshold for that status is $\$20{,}800$.

The income threshold amount refers to the minimum income that requires you to file a tax return. 
Your income of $\$18{,}200$ is *less* than the threshold income, so you are not required to file a tax return.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
If you are an unmarried adult, raising your younger sibling and make   $\$18{,}200$, then you $\boxed{\text{don't have to}}$ file an income tax return.

assessment_items.question_content:
When it's time to file a tax return, you will use a tax form to report all your income and calculate if you owe taxes or are getting a refund.

**If you are a US citizen and work full time, which tax form would you use?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: form 1040 and schedules C or D

assessment_items.question_content:
When it's time to file a tax return, you will use a tax form to report all your income and calculate if you owe taxes or are getting a refund.

>You are a $66$-year-old US citizen and work part-time.

**Which tax form would you use?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: form 1040 and schedules C or D

assessment_items.question_content:
When it's time to file a tax return, you will use a tax form to report all your income and calculate if you owe taxes or are getting a refund.

> You are not a US citizen and are working in the United States temporarily.

**Which tax form would you use?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: form 1040 and schedules C or D
